task_completion.description: Create a new Windows Forms App project named 'TemperatureConverter' at the specified path.
task_completion.visual_evidence: Visual Studio Solution Explorer shows a project named 'TemperatureConverter', or the project creation dialog shows the name 'TemperatureConverter' and Location 'C:\Users\Docker\source\repos\'.

task_completion.description: The main form must be titled 'Temperature Converter'.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The Form Designer window shows a form with the text 'Temperature Converter' in its top title bar, or the Properties window for the form shows the 'Text' property set to 'Temperature Converter'.

task_completion.description: The form contains labels for 'Celsius:' and 'Fahrenheit:', and a button labeled 'Convert'.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The Form Designer shows two Label controls displaying 'Celsius:' and 'Fahrenheit:', and a Button control displaying the text 'Convert'.

task_completion.description: The form contains an input TextBox named 'txtCelsius' and a read-only output TextBox named 'txtFahrenheit'.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The Properties window (or Form1.Designer.cs code) shows two TextBoxes with their '(Name)' properties set to 'txtCelsius' and 'txtFahrenheit'. The 'ReadOnly' property for 'txtFahrenheit' must be set to True (it may appear greyed out in the designer).

task_completion.description: Implement logic to convert Celsius to Fahrenheit using the formula (C * 9/5 + 32) upon clicking 'btnConvert'.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The code editor (Form1.cs) shows an event handler for the button (e.g., btnConvert_Click) that parses the value from txtCelsius, applies the math formula (multiplying by 9/5 or 1.8 and adding 32), and assigns the result to txtFahrenheit.Text.
The Output window at the bottom of Visual Studio displays a message indicating the build was successful, such as 'Build: 1 succeeded' or 'Build succeeded'.

integrity.description: Agent used the Visual Studio 2022 GUI to create the project and design the form.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show the actual Visual Studio IDE interface (Solution Explorer, Form Designer, Properties window) being interacted with, rather than terminal commands or a basic text editor.

integrity.description: The conversion logic is written in the C# code-behind file.
integrity.visual_evidence: The code is visible within the Visual Studio code editor with proper C# syntax, class structures, and using directives, not just pasted as plain text.

integrity.description: The logic calculates the value dynamically rather than hardcoding a specific answer.
integrity.visual_evidence: The code shows variables and the conversion formula being applied to the input, rather than a fixed string assignment like txtFahrenheit.Text = "32";.

privileged_info_for_vlm: Project Name: TemperatureConverter. Expected Path: C:\Users\Docker\source\repos\TemperatureConverter. TextBox 1 Name: txtCelsius. TextBox 2 Name: txtFahrenheit. Button Name: btnConvert.
